findings.title: _subquery_counter is a module-level mutable global — cross-request alias collision
findings.description: _subquery_counter[0] is a module-level integer that increments monotonically across every parse call, never reset. With a single worker this is only aesthetic (aliases like __sub_1__, __sub_2__ keep incrementing across requests), but the real risk is correctness: if two sources upload SQL with unnamed subqueries, the counter does not reset between refreshes. Aliases from source A's parse bleed into source B's parse in the same process. If _resolve_temp_views is called on edges from source B, the subquery alias set may accidentally match alias names that were registered during source A's parse (because both are module-global). The probability of accidental collision scales with number of subqueries across all sources. With multi-worker deployments (Railway can scale to N workers without the user being notified) each worker has its own counter starting at 1, so aliases reset on worker assignment — the aliases are therefore worker-scoped but not request-scoped.
findings.exploit_path: Upload source A (many subqueries, forces counter to 9000+). Upload source B with a subquery that happens to have the same alias __sub_9001__. refresh(B) uses subquery_aliases set containing __sub_9001__; _resolve_temp_views will short-circuit edges through that alias even if __sub_9001__ refers to a different physical subquery whose edges are in state.lineage_graph from source A.
findings.impact: Wrong lineage result — edges from source A's subqueries collapse into source B's lineage graph.
findings.fix: Reset the counter to 0 at the start of each parse_sql() call (top-level entry only, not recursive). Alternatively use a call-local counter passed as a parameter.

findings.title: parse_warnings never pruned on refresh — unbounded memory growth and stale data exposure
findings.description: state.parse_warnings is a list that is only ever appended to (via .extend). Refreshing a source appends new warnings but never removes old ones for that source. After N refreshes of the same source the list holds N copies of every warning. GET /warnings returns all accumulated warnings, so stale errors from parsed-and-fixed files appear forever. More critically, there is no size cap: a source with 1000 parse warnings refreshed 100 times emits 100,000 warning objects that live in memory for the process lifetime.
findings.exploit_path: Register a source with a deliberately malformed SQL file causing many warnings. Refresh it 1000 times (each refresh is a POST — no rate limiting). GET /warnings returns 1M+ objects. Server OOM.
findings.impact: Memory exhaustion (DoS) and permanently stale warning data misleading users.
findings.fix: Before extend, filter out existing warnings for source_id from state.parse_warnings. Add a hard cap (e.g. 10,000 total warnings) with a sentinel warning when truncated.

findings.title: DELETE /sources tears down graph edges but does NOT prune state.parse_warnings for that source
findings.description: delete_source() removes edges from lineage_graph and raw_graph but never touches state.parse_warnings. Warnings for the deleted source persist indefinitely. A user who deletes a source to remove sensitive file names or error messages from the system will find them still exposed at GET /warnings. This is a data-lifecycle violation even in an internal tool.
findings.exploit_path: Register source A, refresh (generates warnings referencing internal file paths). Delete source A. GET /warnings still returns the deleted source's internal path references.
findings.impact: Information leakage of file paths from deleted sources. Minor but a correctness violation.
findings.fix: In delete_source(), filter state.parse_warnings to remove entries where source_id matches the deleted source.

findings.title: nx.compose() on refresh copies edge data objects by reference — shared mutable state between old and new graph snapshots
findings.description: nx.compose(state.lineage_graph, new_graph) creates a new DiGraph where edge attribute dicts are shallow-copied from both inputs. The 'data' attribute on each edge is a LineageEdge dataclass instance. NetworkX compose does NOT deep-copy attribute dicts — it merges them with dict.update(). In practice this means state.lineage_graph after compose shares LineageEdge object references with the new_graph that was just built. If new_graph is later garbage-collected, the shared objects remain alive because state.lineage_graph holds references. However, the more critical risk is: if any code ever mutates a LineageEdge object in-place (even accidentally), it mutates the same object visible in both graph snapshots simultaneously. The raw_graph stored inside graph.graph['_raw_graph'] is popped before compose (line 205) but the LineageEdge objects it references are the same objects in new_graph's edges. Correctness is currently safe only because LineageEdge appears to be immutable in practice — but there is no enforcement (it is a plain dataclass, not frozen).
findings.exploit_path: Any future code that does edge_data.confidence = 'approximate' on a retrieved edge would corrupt the stored edge silently. Not currently exploitable, but a latent time-bomb.
findings.impact: Silent data corruption if LineageEdge is ever mutated; currently theoretical but unguarded.
findings.fix: Mark LineageEdge as @dataclass(frozen=True) to prevent mutation. This is low-cost and eliminates the entire class of risk.

findings.title: PIVOT edge explosion with no bound — crafted SQL causes O(agg_cols * out_cols) edges per PIVOT, unbounded
findings.description: The PIVOT handler in _process_subquery emits one LineageEdge per (agg_col, out_col) pair. For a PIVOT with A aggregate function columns and V IN-list values, it emits A*V edges. There is no deduplication, cap, or guard. A contrived but valid SQL file with 100 PIVOT statements each having 10 agg columns and 100 IN values produces 100 * 10 * 100 = 100,000 edges from one file. The graph grows unboundedly. Additionally, pivot.sql(dialect='databricks') is called once per PIVOT to populate expression — this materializes the full SQL text of the PIVOT clause as a string and stores it in every emitted edge. For a PIVOT IN-list with 1000 values, each of 1000 edges stores a ~10KB string. That is 10MB per PIVOT statement times 100 statements = 1GB in RAM from one file.
findings.exploit_path: Upload a ZIP containing a single .sql file with: SELECT * FROM (SELECT a,b,c,d,e FROM t) PIVOT(SUM(a) AS s1, COUNT(b) AS s2, AVG(c) AS s3, MAX(d) AS s4, MIN(e) AS s5 FOR col IN ('v1','v2',...,'v200')) — repeated 50 times with semicolons. That's 5 aggs * 200 values * 50 statements = 50,000 edges, each storing the full PIVOT SQL (~5KB) = ~250MB of edge data. Server OOM within a single POST /sources/{id}/refresh.
findings.impact: Memory exhaustion / DoS via a single crafted SQL file.
findings.fix: Cap edges emitted per PIVOT (e.g. max 500). Truncate the expression string stored per edge (e.g. max 2KB). Add a per-file edge count limit in _parse_file.

findings.title: WHERE clause filter edge explosion — O(cols_in_predicate) edges per statement, no dedup across UNION branches
findings.description: Filter edges are deduplicated within a single SELECT node via seen_filter_src. However, a UNION ALL with 50 branches sharing the same WHERE predicate (possible if branches are identical) emits the same filter edges 50 times because each branch runs _parse_select_node independently and seen_filter_src is local. More critically: a WHERE clause of the form col1=1 AND col2=2 AND ... AND col100=100 emits 100 filter edges per SELECT, each edge storing the full WHERE SQL text as expression. With 1000 UNION branches, that's 100,000 filter edges per statement, each holding the full WHERE string. No global cap exists.
findings.exploit_path: SQL: INSERT INTO t SELECT a FROM s WHERE c1=1 AND c2=2 AND ... AND c50=50 UNION ALL SELECT a FROM s WHERE c1=1 AND ... AND c50=50 [repeated 200 times]. Emits 200*50 = 10,000 filter edges per statement. A file with 100 such statements emits 1M filter edges.
findings.impact: Memory exhaustion / DoS. Also pollutes downstream lineage queries with synthetic __filter__ nodes.
findings.fix: Deduplicate filter edges globally after _parse_single_statement. Add per-file edge count limit.

findings.title: _lookup in _resolve_temp_views uses src_tbl not in temp_views (not temp_views_lower) for wildcard branch
findings.description: In _lookup(), the wildcard expansion branch checks: 'if src_tbl not in temp_views'. But temp_views was the original set passed to _resolve_temp_views (mixed case). The surrounding code normalizes everything to lowercase via temp_views_lower, and all keys in tv_sources are lowercased. However, the guard on line 739 uses the non-lowercased temp_views. If a temp view name was registered with mixed case (e.g. 'MyView') and the source column is 'myview.*' (lowercased), then src_tbl='myview' and 'myview' is NOT in temp_views (which contains 'MyView'). The wildcard branch returns None (no expansion) and falls through to return the raw source edge, bypassing temp view resolution. The result is stale temp-view edges leaking into the final lineage graph.
findings.exploit_path: CREATE TEMP VIEW MyView AS SELECT ... — SQLGlot normalizes the name to 'myview' in _qualified_table_name if the dialect lowercases it, but if it doesn't, the name stays 'MyView'. Then SELECT * FROM myview (reference in lowercase) causes _detect_temp_views to add 'MyView', but _resolve_temp_views lowercases the lookup key to 'myview'. The guard check 'myview' not in {'MyView'} is True, so wildcard branch is skipped. Real upstream edges are not substituted.
findings.impact: Wrong lineage: temp view not resolved, showing phantom intermediate nodes.
findings.fix: Change line 739 to: if src_tbl not in temp_views_lower: (use the already-computed lowercased set).

findings.title: _resolve_temp_views iterative chain resolution can produce duplicate sources when a temp view appears in its own expansion
findings.description: The iterative chain resolution loop expands tv_sources[tv_col] by replacing any source that is itself a temp view with its upstreams. The loop runs max(len(temp_views)+1) iterations. If temp view A depends on temp view B and temp view B's sources include temp view A (a cycle — which build_graph_with_warnings detects and warns about but does NOT prevent), the expansion loop will keep appending to tv_sources[tv_col] each iteration. After max_iterations the list will contain duplicated sources. The final resolved edges will contain duplicate LineageEdge objects pointing to the same source/target pair.
findings.exploit_path: SQL: CREATE TEMP VIEW a AS SELECT x FROM b; CREATE TEMP VIEW b AS SELECT x FROM a; INSERT INTO t SELECT x FROM b; — cycle between two temp views. The iterative loop expands b's sources to [a.x], then a's sources to [b.x], then b.x is still in temp_views_lower, so expands again... but max_iterations caps at 3 (2 temp views + 1). After 3 iterations, tv_sources has duplicated sources but the loop exits. The final graph gets duplicate edges.
findings.impact: Duplicate edges in lineage graph causing inflated edge counts and incorrect impact analysis (affected_count double-counted).
findings.fix: Deduplicate expanded sources list: expanded = list(dict.fromkeys(expanded)). Also consider detecting temp view cycles before resolution.

findings.title: Refresh does not atomic-swap the graph — queries during refresh see a torn, partially-updated graph
findings.description: refresh_source() first removes old edges from state.lineage_graph (lines 207-223), then composes the new graph in (lines 225-226). Between those two steps, state.lineage_graph is in an inconsistent state: old edges are gone, new edges are not yet present. Any concurrent GET /lineage, GET /tables, GET /impact, or GET /search request hitting during this window will see an incomplete graph. With FastAPI's default single-threaded async event loop, this is safe only if the operations between the two steps have no await points — which they don't. However, if uvicorn is run with --workers N (multiple processes), this is not an issue because each process has its own state. But if a thread-based solution is used, or if the code is ever moved to async, this becomes a real torn-read window.
findings.exploit_path: With a single uvicorn worker: refresh_source() is a synchronous function, so FastAPI runs it in a threadpool executor. Two concurrent HTTP requests to refresh the same source_id can interleave at OS thread context switches, causing both to remove-then-add simultaneously. The second compose() overwrites the first's result silently.
findings.impact: Transient wrong results during concurrent refresh; with multiple concurrent refreshes of the same source, the final graph may be missing edges from one of the refreshes.
findings.fix: Build new_graph fully, then in a single atomic step: swap state.lineage_graph = compose(state.lineage_graph_minus_old_source, new_graph). Use a per-source lock or a single write lock around the mutation window.

findings.title: _collect_union_selects infinite recursion on deeply nested UNION trees
findings.description: _collect_union_selects is a recursive function that walks Union/Intersect/Except trees. SQLGlot represents UNION ALL chains as a right-skewed binary tree. A SQL file with UNION ALL of N branches creates a tree of depth N. For N=10000 (valid SQL, just unusual), the Python default recursion limit of 1000 will be exceeded, raising RecursionError. The RecursionError propagates up through _parse_single_statement and _parse_file where it is caught by the broad except Exception as exc, converting it to a ParseWarning. The file is silently skipped with a warning — not a crash. However, the stack unwind from depth-10000 recursion is slow.
findings.exploit_path: SQL file containing: SELECT 1 UNION ALL SELECT 2 UNION ALL ... UNION ALL SELECT 10000 (10000 branches). _collect_union_selects recurses 10000 levels deep, hits Python's recursion limit, throws RecursionError.
findings.impact: File silently dropped from lineage with a parse warning; slow stack unwind. Not a crash due to broad except, but correctness is lost for the file.
findings.fix: Rewrite _collect_union_selects as an iterative stack-based traversal using an explicit list stack instead of Python call stack recursion.

findings.title: Git source type: token embedded in URL passed to ls_remote — token visible in git error messages
findings.description: When registering a git source with a PAT token, the token is embedded into the URL: auth_url = url.replace('https://', 'https://{token}@', 1). This auth_url is passed to git.cmd.Git().ls_remote(auth_url). If ls_remote fails with a GitCommandError, line 101 returns exc.stderr.strip() in the HTTP response. Git's stderr for an auth failure often echoes the URL including the embedded token (e.g. 'fatal: unable to access https://ghp_TOKEN@github.com/...: ...'). The code tries to sanitize by saying 'Sanitize: never echo back the token-embedded URL' but it only suppresses the auth_url from the direct message — exc.stderr from git itself may still contain it.
findings.exploit_path: POST /sources with source_type=git, url=https://github.com/nonexistent/repo, token=MY_SECRET_TOKEN. Git fails; exc.stderr contains 'fatal: repository https://MY_SECRET_TOKEN@github.com/nonexistent/repo not found'. HTTP 400 response body leaks MY_SECRET_TOKEN.
findings.impact: Token leakage in error response. Internal tool so no external attacker, but token logging in Railway/server logs is a real operational risk.
findings.fix: Sanitize exc.stderr by replacing the token with '***' before returning: sanitized = (exc.stderr or '').replace(token, '***') if token else exc.stderr or str(exc).

findings.title: search endpoint: no limit on results — full graph scan returned for single-character queries
findings.description: GET /search?q=a iterates every node in state.lineage_graph and returns all matches. For a large graph with 500,000 column nodes, this returns a JSON array with 500,000 entries (or close to it for common column name fragments). No pagination, no limit, no short-circuit. The response body could be hundreds of MB.
findings.exploit_path: GET /search?q=. (dot matches everything since it's substring search, not regex). Returns every node in the graph.
findings.impact: Large response causing client OOM or very slow rendering. Server RAM unaffected (just serialization cost), but client and network are impacted.
findings.fix: Add a hard limit: if len(results) > 500: break. Return a 'truncated' flag.

findings.title: _trace_raw_paths wildcard expansion in get_preds may generate O(N^2) pred_named nodes for a wide wildcard fan-out
findings.description: In get_preds(), the wildcard expansion checks if tbl.* has predecessors in raw_graph and synthesizes named nodes. If tbl.* has K predecessors each of which are also wildcards (pred_wc.endswith('.*')), K synthetic named nodes are created per call. With max_depth=10 and branching factor K, DFS explores K^10 paths total (before truncation at max_paths=50). The max_paths=50 truncation prevents full explosion, but the work done before reaching 50 paths can be significant for wide wildcard fans.
findings.exploit_path: A SQL file with SELECT * FROM (SELECT * FROM (... )) 10 levels deep with 10 source tables at each level. The raw_graph will have wildcard edges at each level; _trace_raw_paths DFS explores 10^10 partial paths before truncating.
findings.impact: Slow response for GET /lineage/paths — potential request timeout or high CPU spike.
findings.fix: The max_depth=10 cap mostly protects here (K^10 is bounded by 10^10 which is too large, but truncated). Consider lowering max_depth to 6 or adding a node-visit counter cap across all DFS branches.

residual_risks.risk: ingest_databricks is not reviewed here (file not read). If it downloads large notebook files without size checks, similar PIVOT/filter edge explosion risks apply. The HTTP 400 catch-all for Databricks auth failure hides the real exception — a network timeout would also surface as 'Databricks authentication failed' making debugging hard.

residual_risks.area: ZIP bomb / path traversal in ingest_zip
residual_risks.risk: ingest_zip not reviewed. A ZIP with 50MB compressed, 5GB uncompressed (zip bomb) would expand to fill disk. A ZIP with ../../../etc/passwd as a file path could write to arbitrary locations. Standard risks for ZIP handling.
ingest_git clones a full repo. A repo with 10GB of history causes Railway disk exhaustion. No clone depth or size limit visible from routes.py.

nx.compose() for a very large graph (millions of edges) is O(N) but returns a new graph object. Memory usage momentarily doubles during compose as both old and new graphs co-exist. Under memory pressure this could OOM even if the final graph would fit.

testing_gaps: No test for PIVOT with large IN-lists (>50 values) to verify edge count and memory behavior.
No test for parse_warnings accumulation across multiple refreshes of the same source.
No test for delete_source followed by GET /warnings to verify stale warnings are pruned.
No test for UNION ALL with >1000 branches to verify _collect_union_selects does not hit Python recursion limit.
No test for mixed-case temp view names to catch the src_tbl not in temp_views (vs temp_views_lower) bug in _lookup.
No test for concurrent refresh of the same source_id to verify graph consistency.
No test for git token leakage in error responses.
No test for GET /search with a single-character query to verify response size is bounded.
No test for the _subquery_counter not resetting between parse_sql() calls across different source uploads.
